Q1: Honestly, my study routine was all over the place. I'd sit down with the best intentions, planning to go through lecture recordings and readings, but then I'd get overwhelmed by how much there was across all sixteen courses. I tried to make schedules, color-coded calendars, the whole thing, but I could never stick to them. Most of my studying happened in these frantic bursts when I realized I was falling behind, which was basically always.
Q2: My pattern was completely driven by panic and deadlines. There was no regular weekly rhythm at all. Some weeks I'd be up until 3am trying to catch up on one course, then completely neglect three others. With so many online courses running simultaneously, I was constantly putting out fires rather than actually learning systematically. I kept telling myself next week would be different, but it never was.
Q3: Usually it was either guilt or fear that prompted me to log in. I'd see an email notification about a deadline or remember that I hadn't checked in for days, and this wave of anxiety would hit me. Sometimes I'd log in just to feel like I was doing something productive, even if I ended up just clicking around without really engaging. The gamified tasks they added actually helped sometimes because earning points felt like a small win when everything else felt impossible.
Q4: For maybe two or three weeks early on, I got into a groove of checking the LMS every morning with my coffee. It felt manageable then, like I was on top of things. But once assignments started piling up and I fell behind in a few courses, that routine completely collapsed. After that, logging in became more of a source of stress than a habit, so I'd avoid it even though I knew that made things worse.
Q5: The lecture recordings were my lifeline since I learn best by listening. I'd often play them while doing other things or replay difficult sections multiple times. The discussion boards were also helpful when other students explained concepts in their own words—sometimes that clicked better than the professor's explanation. The gamified tasks gave me small achievable goals which felt good when everything else seemed insurmountable.
Q6: I basically ignored most of the readings and supplementary PDFs. There were just too many of them, and reading on a screen exhausted me. I also rarely touched the practice quizzes until right before exams, which was obviously a mistake. I kept telling myself I'd get to them later, but later never came because something more urgent always took priority.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate results or if it directly connected to an upcoming assessment. The gamified elements helped because getting points or badges gave me a sense of progress even when I was struggling. If something felt abstract or disconnected from what I'd be tested on, I couldn't justify spending time on it when I was already drowning.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. Required work already consumed more time than I had, so optional stuff got mentally filed under 'nice to have but not happening.' There was also less guilt attached to skipping optional things, which made them easy to deprioritize. Sometimes I'd feel sad about missing out on what might have been interesting content, but survival mode doesn't leave room for exploration.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a ton of lecture recordings and listened to them on my phone during my commute and while exercising. I also had a study group that met at the library where we'd work through problems on whiteboards and quiz each other verbally. None of that would show up anywhere in the system. I probably did more learning in those offline sessions than in front of my computer.
Q10: My study group sessions were huge for me—we'd spend hours discussing concepts, teaching each other, working through practice problems together. I also recorded myself explaining topics and played them back, which really helped me process information as an auditory learner. Sometimes I'd pause a video after five minutes and spend an hour thinking through the concepts, but the LMS would only show five minutes of watch time.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture or PDF once, then work with it for days offline. I had folders full of course materials on my laptop that I'd study from repeatedly without ever going back to the LMS. It was actually easier to focus without the internet tempting me with distractions. The LMS would show one download, but that single file might represent ten hours of study.
Q12: Around week six, everything crashed. I had major deadlines in multiple courses simultaneously, plus my extracurriculars were demanding more time. My anxiety spiked to the point where I'd feel physically sick looking at my to-do list. I started avoiding the LMS entirely for days at a time because opening it made me feel like a failure. The sadness just kind of settled in and wouldn't lift.
Q13: Massively. When I was exhausted or stressed, I'd log into the LMS and just stare at the screen without absorbing anything. The cognitive overload from managing sixteen courses meant I was constantly context-switching and never getting deep into any material. Some weeks my part-time job and club commitments left me with almost no energy for academics. I'd open videos but be too tired to actually process what I was hearing.
Q14: More times than I can count. I'd have a lecture playing while my mind was completely elsewhere, worrying about other deadlines or just zoning out from exhaustion. Sometimes I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just to feel like I was doing something. My eyes would move across the screen but nothing was actually sinking in. It was like going through the motions of studying without any actual learning happening.
Q15: I avoided the big assignments constantly, even though I knew they were killing my grade. The anxiety around them was so intense that I'd do anything else instead—check discussions, rewatch old lectures, organize my notes. Starting a major assignment meant confronting how far behind I was, and that felt unbearable. By the time I finally started, there was never enough time to do quality work.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to submit anything. My LMS usage would spike dramatically in the 48 hours before something was due. I'd cram everything into these desperate last-minute sessions, which meant the quality was never what it could have been. The constant deadline pressure kept me in a perpetual state of anxiety, which made it even harder to work steadily throughout the term.
Q17: When I got feedback, it was often too late to really help me improve for that course. Sometimes positive feedback motivated me briefly, but negative feedback often sent me into a spiral where I'd avoid that course for days. I appreciated the feedback intellectually but emotionally it was hard to handle when I was already feeling like a failure. The gamified task feedback was actually easier to take because it felt lower stakes.
Q18: Having sixteen courses meant the structure of any single course almost didn't matter—I was just trying to survive the aggregate workload. Courses with more flexible deadlines got pushed aside for ones with strict due dates. The ones with clear, modular structures were easier to dip in and out of. But honestly, even well-designed courses suffered because I couldn't give any of them proper attention.
Q19: Courses that broke content into small, manageable chunks were easier to engage with when I only had thirty minutes. The gamified elements helped because they gave me achievable micro-goals. But courses with long, dense lectures or vague assignment descriptions made my anxiety worse. When I couldn't figure out exactly what was expected, I'd freeze up and avoid the work entirely.
Q20: That's basically my story. I was logging in constantly, watching videos, reading discussions, clicking around—but I couldn't bring myself to complete and submit the actual assignments. The work felt too overwhelming, or I'd start something and realize I didn't understand the prerequisites, or I'd run out of time because I'd spent hours on other courses. High activity doesn't mean high output when you're drowning.
Q21: I can see how that would happen if someone studies efficiently offline or already knows the material. That wasn't me though—I needed more engagement, not less, but I couldn't translate my high LMS activity into actual performance. Maybe some people can watch a lecture once, get it, and ace the exam without showing much platform activity. I envy those people honestly.
Q22: Course logs can show when and how often you access things, which gives a decent picture of attendance patterns. But they completely miss the quality of engagement—whether you're actually understanding or just going through motions. They miss offline studying, group work, the mental processing that happens away from the screen. And they definitely can't capture the emotional experience of learning, the anxiety and overwhelm that shaped everything I did.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about and connecting with the material, not just completing tasks. It's when you're curious, asking questions, making connections to other things you know. This semester, I wanted to be engaged—I signed up for all these courses because I was interested—but the sheer volume made real engagement impossible. I was going through motions instead of actually learning.
Q24: The LMS can't show how hard I was trying or how much I wanted to succeed. It can't capture the hours I spent listening to recordings offline, the study group sessions, the genuine interest I had in the material that got buried under anxiety. It doesn't show that I cried out of frustration multiple times or that I developed insomnia from stress. The data shows a failing student, but it doesn't show someone who was fighting as hard as they could and still drowning.
